data.title: Cool Inflation in France, Spain Hints at Fresh Cuts Ahead — Update
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

Low inflation in major European economies this month points to a cooling in price rises across the wider eurozone and tees up fresh cuts to interest rates in the months ahead.

French consumer prices were 0.9% higher on year this month, the same rate as in February, according to EU-harmonized figures set out Friday by the French statistics agency. In Spain, annual inflation dropped to 2.2% in March from 2.9% a month earlier, separate figures showed.

The French rate remains well below the 2% that constitutes policymakers' benchmark. Still, the headline rate was kept lower by falling energy prices, while services inflation picked up pace over the month, the agency said. In Spain, core inflation also fell, suggesting underlying price pressures are easing.

That suggests inflation will have cooled this month in the eurozone from the 2.3% annual rate booked in February, and makes the European Central Bank more likely to cut interest rates further this year. The ECB reduced its key rate to 2.5% this month, a sixth move to lower borrowing costs in nine months.

The eurozone is experiencing weak economic growth, a situation compounded by threats of U.S. tariffs on goods including metals and automobiles. The extent and effect of those tariffs remain unclear and given that uncertainty, the ECB's correct response to slow growth isn't clear, Bank of France Governor Francois Villeroy de Galhau said this week. A fresh cut at next month's meeting of the governing council won't be an automatic choice, he said.

That ambiguity was echoed by Luis de Guindos, the bank's vice-president. "It is difficult to say at the moment what we will do" in April, De Guindos told a conference hosted by the Institute of International Finance on Thursday.

Nevertheless, investors are widely expecting the bank to cut again at April's meeting, according to data provided by LSEG Refinitiv.

Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby

data.title: Italian police arrest 'Breaking Bad' fan in meth lab bust
data.description:
Italian police said on Friday they had dismantled one of the largest clandestine methamphetamine laboratories in the country, arresting a university student they described as a fan of the cult TV series "Breaking Bad".

The show chronicles the downward spiral of a high-school chemistry teacher who uses his knowledge to make and sell methamphetamine, also known as crystal meth.

A 22-year-old from the northern city of Novara was charged with the illegal production of drugs, police said in a statement. In Italy, the crime carries sentences ranging from six to 20 years' imprisonment.

Officers who raided a property on March 21 found a large table with "a complex distillation workshop" on the ground floor, lit by a dim red light and including flasks, glass tubes and heat resistant ampoules.

In the attic, they found an area where drug compounds such as MDMA and DMT were mixed and prepared, as well as "hallucinogenic stamps" - small pieces of paper doused in drugs such as LSD.

data.title: F&G Annuities & Life Insider Bought Shares Worth $359,618, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
Christopher O Blunt, Director, Chief Executive Officer, on March 27, 2025, executed a purchase for 10,000 shares in F&G Annuities & Life for $359,618. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, Blunt has control over a total of 849,584 common shares of the company, with 849,584 shares held directly.

SEC Filing:

data.title: UK's SSE gains on naming new CEO
data.description:
** SSE rises as much as 1.94% to 1,576p; top gainer on FTSE 100 index CURRENCYCOM:UK100

** British power generator and network operator names company veteran Martin Pibworth as its Chief Executive designate to replace outgoing boss Alistair Phillips-Davies

** Pibworth, currently the chief commercial officer, will take over after co's AGM in July; Davies will leave in November

** Jefferies says, "We wouldn't expect the company to undergo any major strategic review with him as the new CEO"

** Stock falls ~3.6% YTD

data.title: Wai Chun Bio-Technology to Vote April 30 on Capital Reorganization
data.description:
Wai Chun Bio-Technology will hold an extraordinary general meeting on April 30 to vote on the company's proposed capital reorganization.

The biotech firm is planning a capital reorganization in which it will reduce the par value of each of its issued ordinary share to HK$0.01 from HK$0.25, according to a Thursday bourse filing.

The company will reduce its issued share capital by cancelling the paid-up capital of HK$0.25 on each issued ordinary share.

Following the reduction, each authorized but unissued ordinary and preference share with a par value of HK$0.25 will be divided into 25 new shares with a par value of HK$0.01 each.

After the reorganization, the company's authorized capital will become 10 billion new ordinary shares, totaling HK$100 million, and 204 million new preference shares, amounting to HK$2.04 million.

data.title: WH Smith to Sell UK High Street Business in GBP76 Million Deal
data.description:
WH Smith agreed to sell its UK high street business to Modella Capital at an enterprise value of 76 million pounds sterling.

The travel retailer will receive gross cash proceeds of 52 million pounds and expects to receive net cash proceeds of 25 million pounds, according to a Friday filing. The sold business has 480 stores across the UK.

WH Smith said it will become a pure play global travel retailer following the sale, which is expected to be completed in the fourth quarter of the company's current fiscal year.

data.title: Ethernity Networks Shares Jump on Aerospace System Contract Extension
data.description:
By Ian Walker

Ethernity Networks shares rose after the company said that it signed an extension to a contract with a tier 1 U.S.-based aerospace system products provider valued at $290,000.

London-listed shares were up 0.01 pence, or 17%, at 0.04 pence in early morning trading. However, they are currently down 68% over the year to date.

Ethernity Networks--which supplies data processing chip technology for networking appliances--said Friday that the extension takes the contract value to $1.34 million.

Ethernity added that it expects further revenue from the unnamed customer during the second half of this year.

Write to Ian Walker at ian.walker@wsj.com

data.title: Guangxi Energy Investing 312 Million Yuan in Laibin Solar Power Project
data.description:
Guangxi Energy plans to invest 311.6 million yuan in a 94.1-megawatt photovoltaic power project in Laibin City in China's Guangxi province.

The project, developed by subsidiary Qiaogong Energy, will cover 1,348 mu and include a 110-kilovolt booster station.

Construction is expected to take 10 months, with an estimated first-year power generation of 100.4 million kilowatt-hours.

Shares of the electricity service provider closed 10% lower Friday.

data.title: DNO makes North Sea gas and oil discovery
data.description:
Norwegian oil and gas operator DNO and its partners have made an oil and gas discovery in the "Kjottkake" prospect in the North Sea, the Norwegian Offshore Directorate said on Friday.

The authority said preliminary calculations indicate that the discovery holds recoverable reserves of between 38.4 and 74.2 million barrels of oil equivalent.

"The licensees will consider a potential development and further follow-up of the discovery alongside other discoveries and prospects in the vicinity," it said in a statement.

DNO holds a 40% stake in the exploration permit while Aker BP holds 30% and Japex Norway and Concedo each hold 15%.

data.title: Kone Cut to Hold From Buy by Jefferies
Hong Kong Shares Fall; Declines Led by Property, Pharma Stocks — Market Talk
data.description: Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index closed 0.65% lower at 23426.60, weighed by property and pharmaceutical stocks. Market focus remained on U.S. President Trump's reciprocal tariffs, ING's economists and analysts said in a note. Investors also awaited China's PMI readings for March due next week as it may provide signs into the economy's performance. Among best performers, Country Garden Services climbed 8.7%, Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group rose 6.55% and CSPC Pharmaceutical Group was up 2.85%. Meanwhile, Haier Smart Home dropped 7.5% and SMIC lost 5.0%. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0738 GMT - Irish government bonds could stay weak relative to eurozone peers as the country's economy is exposed to tariff risks more than many others. "Among eurozone government bonds, we think tariff exposure is partly behind the underperformance of Irish credit this year given U.S. pharmaceutical companies' manufacturing there," Citi Research's Aman Bansal says in a note. Citi is long in Irish bonds but acknowledges that the tariff exposure might prevent a meaningful outperformance of the credit while the uncertainty lingers. "In contrast, Spain, Greece and France might be relatively shielded from U.S. reciprocal tariff impact due to their smaller export exposure," the rates strategist says. The 10-year Irish bond yield falls 3 basis point to 3.035%; the 10-year Bund yield falls 4.3bps to 2.734%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0222 GMT - IHH Healthcare's Turkish unit is likely to maintain its growth momentum despite political uncertainty there, Maybank Investment Bank analysts Nur Natasha Ariza and Yin Shao Yang say in a note. While accounting adjustments related to hyperinflation may weigh on reported earnings amid the Turkish lira's depreciation, core earnings should remain resilient, they say. The Acibadem unit benefits from foreign-currency-denominated medical tourism and steady private healthcare spending, they say. Contributing 30% of IHH's 2024 revenue, the Turkey business is projected to see 9% Ebitda growth in 2025, helped partly by stable in-patient volumes, they add. The analysts remain positive on Acibadem's non-lira income and IHH's other key segments. Maybank maintains a buy rating on IHH and keeps its target price at MYR7.97. Shares are unchanged at MYR6.91. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2223 GMT - Pathology services provider Healius's target of a high-single-digit Ebit margin by FY 2027 looks ambitious to Jefferies. Healius expects to reach this goal through a mix of revenue and cost initiatives. However, analyst David Stanton forecasts Healius achieving a 4.4% margin in FY 2028. "Growth in Ebit margin is not expected to be linear in the timeframe--we believe it is likely to be back-ended," Jefferies says. "We estimate that low-end to high-end targets imply FY 2028 Pathology Ebit margins of 7.0% to 8.6%." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2212 GMT - Pathology services provider Healius is in the recovery room, but Barrenjoey is skeptical that the company can reach performance targets laid out at its investor day yesterday. Healius wants to grow revenue at 6% and make a high-single-digit Ebit margin by FY 2027. Analyst Saul Hadassin says this implies Ebit growing at a compound annual rate of 120% from FY 2025-FY 2027. "While we anticipate an improvement in operating performance, this target looks difficult to deliver, particularly in light of looming cuts to VitB12/mid-stream urine tests," Barrenjoey says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1449 GMT - European luxury companies have the capacity and demand to shift production facilities to the U.S. to mitigate the potential hit of tariffs, with Louis Vuitton being an example, Bernstein analysts say. Amid a slowdown in demand for high-end goods, the luxury sector faces a volatile recovery, they say. Recent decisions regarding levies in the U.S.--which has become a key focus for luxury groups given weakness in the key China market--have made the trajectory more uncertain, they say. Companies like EssilorLuxottica--which has a high exposure to U.S. consumers while importing the bulk of its products from China, Mexico, and Europe--could be more exposed, Bernstein adds. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

1344 GMT - Novo Nordisk sees flat U.S. weekly prescription rate growth for its obesity treatment Wegovy due to competition from U.S. rival Eli Lilly and compounding pharmacies, coupled with reimbursement challenges, Morgan Stanley analysts say. The Danish pharmaceutical company could trim its sales guidance, possibly alongside its 1Q results, due to the slower U.S. prescription growth, they say. While long-term forecasts still indicate potential upside, the future of the obesity market, peak sales potential and longevity of Novo Nordisk's weight loss drug portfolio remain uncertain, they add. The analysts also cite Eli Lilly's market share gains in the U.S. and product differentiation, including greater weight loss and better tolerability. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

1138 GMT - The FDA approval of Vykat XR is a significant win for Soleno Therapeutics, say analysts at Stifel, who cite a very favorable and clean label and pricing that came in well above expectations. Stifel reiterates its buy rating on Soleno shares and says it thinks the drug is well positioned to launch strongly into a blockbuster opportunity, given a significant unmet need, a well-diagnosed/concentrated patient population, a favorable risk/benefit and high physician/patient awareness. Stifel, with a $74 target price on Soleno shares, says M&A remains a plausible upside case as well. Soleno surges 37% premarket to $67.22. (colin.kellaher@wsj.com)

1121 GMT - ProSiebenSat.1's share-price development is uncertain but holds a speculative investment opportunity after MFE-MediaForEurope's takeover bid, Equita Research analyst Milo Silvestre says in a note. The media company's agreement to acquire shares from a current shareholder of the German media conglomerate is a step toward surpassing the 30% threshold. This would grant greater flexibility to purchase additional shares without price restrictions, the analyst says. The reaction of other major shareholders, including its second-largest investor PPF, remains to be seen, he says. Shares of ProSiebenSat.1 fall 10% to 5.86 euros, while MFE shares are down 2.2% at 4.72 euros.(helena.smolak@wsj.com)

1105 GMT - MFE-MediaForEurope's bid to raise its ProsiebenSat.1 stake is a strategic move aimed at avoiding a more expensive mandatory takeover offer later, Warburg analyst Joerg Philipp Frey says in a note. MFE has steadily increased its ownership to just below 30%, a threshold that would trigger a mandatory takeover offer under German market rules. The European media group's takeover offer for ProsiebenSat.1 is likely to see low uptake as the proposal is unattractive for most investors, he says. The low offer price--representing a 12% discount to Wednesday's closing price--and the share-based components are key deterrents, Frey says. Shares of ProSiebenSat.1 fall 11% to 5.82 euros, while MFE shares are down 1.7% at 4.74 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

1022 GMT - MFE-MediaForEurope's takeover bid for ProSiebenSat.1 allows the European media group to gain control while delaying a potentially more expensive acquisition until after ProSiebenSat.1 sells its non-core assets, AlphaValue analyst Jean-Michel Salvador says in a note. However, the offer is unfavourable to the German media conglomerate's minority shareholders, priced around 5.75 euros per share, significantly below yesterday's closing price of 6.50 euros, he says. "It is uncertain whether ProSieben shareholders will really benefit from the sale proceeds while being left with a depleted ProSieben that MFE may not acquire at a premium," the analyst says. Shares of ProSiebenSat.1 fall 11% to 5.82 euros, while MFE shares are down 1.7% at 4.74 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0820 GMT - Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index closed 0.65% lower at 23426.60, weighed by property and pharmaceutical stocks. Market focus remained on U.S. President Trump's reciprocal tariffs, ING's economists and analysts said in a note. Investors also awaited China's PMI readings for March due next week as it may provide signs into the economy's performance. Among best performers, Country Garden Services climbed 8.7%, Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group rose 6.55% and CSPC Pharmaceutical Group was up 2.85%. Meanwhile, Haier Smart Home dropped 7.5% and SMIC lost 5.0%. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0732 GMT - There's been a significant improvement in global investors' interest and sentiment toward China equities, UBS Securities China equity strategist Lei Meng says in a research note. The improving investor sentiment reflects China's recent market rally and the confidence boost as a result of China's AI breakthrough and policy easing measures, the analyst says. Global investors are trying to identify investment opportunities from short- and medium-term trends of China's economy rather than long-term dynamics, he says. AI and related industries remain the most attractive themes while consumer names are gaining popularity, they say. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0720 GMT - Chinese shares ended lower in a pullback after the recent rally. Almost all the sectors fell, led by consumer and oil stocks. Yonghui Superstores declined 2.0% and China Tourism Group Duty Free dropped 1.25%. PetroChina put off 1.2% and Cnooc was off by 2.3%. Despite the losses in today's session, UBS analyst Lei Meng notes that global investors' interest and sentiment toward Chinese equities has improved significantly recently, with eyes on AI and related industries. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index ended 0.7% lower at 3351.31, the Shenzhen Composite Index declined 0.9% and the ChiNext Price Index was off by 0.8%. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0714 GMT - Japanese stocks ended lower amid persisting concerns over U.S. tariffs and many companies started trading ex-dividend. Auto and financial stocks led the declines, with Toyota Motor dropping 4.5% and Honda Motor shedding 4.9%, while Nomura Holdings fell 4.7% and Japan Post Bank lost 4.5%. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 1.8% to 37120.33. Investors focused on U.S. trade and foreign policies. The 10-year Japanese government bond yield fell 4 bps to 1.545%. USD/JPY is at 150.62, compared with 151.04 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0659 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening slightly lower with IG calling the OMXS30 down 0.2% at around 2568. Stock markets were hit by tariff news yesterday, with car manufacturers unsurprisingly among the losers, SEB analysts say in a note. U.S. jobless data showed the labor market is still holding up quite well, but SEB notes it is unclear when and to what extent former federal employees who have been laid off by DOGE will begin to appear in the statistics. In Asia, stock markets are under pressure on fears that the U.S. will introduce reciprocal tariffs on April 2, SEB adds. Equity market futures are pointing slightly down in Europe and slightly up in the U.S. OMXS30 closed at 2572.81, OMXN40 at 2442.44 and OBX at 1459.18. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0656 GMT - Singapore's Straits Times Index crossed the 4000-mark in early trade and edged 0.1% lower at 3975.88 Friday afternoon. The benchmark index earlier marked a record-high of 4005.18 before edging lower. The STI is up around 5% year-to-date. Among decliners, DFI Retail Group and Hongkong Land falls 1.5% each, while Seatrium loses 1.4%. Meanwhile, ST Engineering is up 0.9% and ST Engineering adds 0.7%. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1913 GMT - U.S. states and their pension plans are increasingly considering cryptocurrency as areserve investment, S&P Global Ratings says. The recent creation of crypto exchange-traded funds and the evolving regulatory landscape may have increased interest in crypto by helping address associated risks, according to S&P. The agency also says 17 U.S. states either allow or have introduced legislation to allow crypto as a reserve asset, with 16 proceeding toward including crypto in statewide pension trusts. "Although crypto is still a very small allocation in U.S. state reserve andpension holdings, many states are in various stages of implementing policychanges to allow the use of bitcoin or other cryptocurrencies in their generalfund and/or pension trust assets," said S&P Global Ratings credit analyst Todd Kanaster. Earlier this month, President Trump said he would move forward on creating a U.S. crypto strategic reserve. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1408 GMT - The average U.S. homebuyer's monthly payment reached a record $2,807 in late March, up 5.3% from last year, Redfin says. Rising sale prices--up 3% annually--and mortgage rates averaging 6.67%--double pandemic lows--are driving the surge. Though rates fell from January's 7.04%, high costs still stifle sales, with pending home sales down 4.6%. But spring seems to be sparking activity: mortgage applications hit a seasonal peak since February, home tours outpace last year, and "homes for sale" searches have soared. New listings jumped 7.5%, the biggest rise in 2025. If listings grow and rates drop, pending sales could rebound. Redfin agents have said that some buyers are negotiating successfully despite high prices. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1119 GMT - European banks will closely follow a decision on whether to allow Banco BPM to apply the so-called Danish Compromise rule to its acquisition of asset manager Anima, ING analyst Suvi Platerink Kosonen says in a note. The Danish Compromise allows banks to benefit from a more lenient capital treatment when buying asset managers via an insurance subsidiary. The European Central Bank communicated a negative opinion on the application of this rule for BPM's acquisition of Anima, according to the Italian bank, though the European Banking Authority has the final say. "A negative definitive decision on the usage of the Danish Compromise could act as a break for further M&A on the financial sector in our view," the analyst notes. French bank BNP Paribas is also using the Danish Compromise to buy AXA Investment Managers. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0942 GMT - UniCredit could be paradoxically motivated by a rejected application of the "Danish Compromise" rules on the acquisition of asset manager Anima by Banco BPM, which the Italian lender has launched a takeover offer for, Deutsche Bank says in a research note. BPM heard from the European Central Bank that it holds a negative view on the application of the regulation which allows for less capital consumption in such a deal. UniCredit could benefit from the share drop of BPM, reducing the amount of a potential cash bump for a possible bid, analyst Giovanni Razzoli and associate Rishika Garg write. "Furthermore, the uncertain regulatory environment with the DC may deter UniCredit from pursuing other acquisitions reliant on similar capital arbitrage strategies," they say. Both stocks fall, tracking the market. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0910 GMT - Italy's Banco BPM is highly likely to proceed with its offer to buy asset manager Anima even if it can't apply the more lenient Danish Compromise rules and benefit from the lighter capital hit under the European regulation, Equita says in a research note. BPM said the ECB communicated a negative opinion on the application of the rule for this deal, but noted that the European Banking Authority has the final say. The lender has made clear that it could waive one or more effectiveness conditions of the offer. The impact on BPM's CET1 ratio from the acquisition would be higher than 250 basis points without the compromise, compared with less than 40 basis points if it is applied, analyst Andrea Lisi says. Shares slip 0.8%, extending Wednesday's losses. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0839 GMT - NatWest's commercial and institutional unit reflects the strength of its business franchise, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note after the U.K. bank hosted a deep-dive on the division, which it said had a good start of the year. "Clearly subject to wider U.K. economic drivers, management do see particular growth opportunities in the Mid-Market sector where they bank 1 in 4 customers, contributing around a quarter of group income," analysts Edward Firth and Elise Yu Ge write. They note that innovation, social housing and trade finance are particular areas of interest, adding that the recent AI rollout could potentially improve costs while moving towards a model of origination and distribution that is positive for capital efficiency. Shares trade at 456.5 pence and are up 14% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0255 GMT - Overseas oil fields will likely be the key production volume driver for Cnooc in 2025-2026 with projects in Brazil and Guyana, Citi analysts say in a research note. The analysts think Cnooc's 2024 reserve replacement ratio was "impressive" with 11 new discoveries and 30 successful appraisals. Management thinks China's natural gas prices should remain relatively more stable compared to the global market given that all natural gas produced by Cnooc is consumed domestically and its prices are not directly tied to international natural gas prices, the analysts note. Citi maintains a buy rating on Cnooc and keeps its target price at HK$24.00. Shares are last at 1.4% lower at HK$18.40. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0215 GMT - RHB IB downgrades Malaysia's oil & gas sector to neutral from overweight, citing prolonged uncertainties in the industry. Brent crude may remain at $75/bbl in 2025-2026, with OPEC+ gradually increasing output from April, analyst Sean Lim says in a note. The oil market's deficit could narrow to 0.7mbpd in 2025 from 1.4mbpd in 2024, and may turn into a surplus by 2026 if production hikes continue, he reckons. Geopolitical risks are in focus, with regional tensions and U.S. sanctions affecting supply, he says. Domestically, Petronas' shift to half-yearly financial reporting from a quarterly basis, cost-cutting measures, and uncertainties surrounding capital expenditure add to the sector's challenges, he says. RHB favors upstream service providers with strong maintenance exposure and global diversification for more stable earnings. The brokerage names Bumi Armada, Yinson Holdings, and Dayang Enterprise as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0049 GMT - Oil prices are mixed in early Asian trade. The oil market could see growing volatility and pullback risks, says Maria Agustina Patti, a financial markets strategist consultant at Exness. Prices could be supported by tighter global supply concerns, driven by U.S. tariff threats on Venezuelan oil buyers and ongoing sanctions on Iranian oil, Patti says in an email. Traders may stay cautious about any developments that may exacerbate supply disruptions, Patti adds. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are flat at $69.89/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are down 0.1% at $73.98/bbl.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

1920 GMT - Fiber operations are a big opportunity for telecommunications companies to capitalize on the rise of data-center construction to power artificial intelligence, Citizens analyst Greg Miller tells WSJ. Data centers are increasingly being built in remote areas like North Dakota and Alabama, where there is more power available for them, but those centers need a lot of fiber to connect them back to a broader network, Miller says. Telecom companies are trying to get ahead of this: Zayo just bought Crown Castle's fiber segment and AT&T is reportedly in talks with Lumen Technologies to buy its fiber unit, following a deal for Lumen to provide fiber for Microsoft's data centers. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1909 GMT - Crude futures settle higher with the market weighing the impact of potential supply disruptions against the economic impact of U.S. tariffs. The gradual rally from the March 5 low of $65.22 for WTI could be a mixture of genuine supply concerns around Canadian, Mexican and Venezuelan oil and a technical bounce from a oversold market, Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note. "But the rally may be flying a little too close to tariff demand destruction and the OPEC+ production increases that are set to start in April," he adds. WTI settles up 0.4% at $69.92 a barrel, and Brent rises 0.3% to $74.03 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1350 GMT - Oil futures are trading near flat with the impending threat of U.S. trade tariffs weighing against the supply concerns around U.S. sanctions on Iranian and Venezuelan crude. Futures are trying to hold this week's $2 a barrel rally spurred by the U.S. sanctions "versus the new tariffs that are supposed to come into the market next week," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. The WTI May contract is testing resistance at the 50-day and 200-day moving averages around $70.09 and $70.50, he says. "A solid close above turns the longer-term trend back to the bulls and targets the $73 area." WTI is off 0.1% at $69.59 a barrel, and Brent is down 0.2% at $73.66 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1050 GMT - Repsol's joint venture with NEO Energy makes sense given the U.K. government's fiscal policy on oil and gas developments, RBC Capital Markets analysts Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. The joint venture continues a trend seen across U.K. North Sea exposed companies because it allows operators to pool resources and allocate less capital in the region while continuing to grow, they write. Repsol's 2024 capital expenditure in the U.K. was around 200 million euros and the entity is expected to be de-consolidated on the deal closing, the analysts write. Shares trade up 0.7% at 12.50 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0230 GMT - China Sunsine Chemical's sales volumes are likely to sustain an uptrend, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report, as they raise its target price for the stock to S$0.63 from S$0.58, maintaining a buy rating. The rubber chemicals producer's sales in China are expected to improve, supported by the country's targeted stimulus measures to aid consumption recovery, the analysts say. Also, according to Tireworld, China's tire industry data service provider, the nation's rubber tire exports grew 3.3% on year in January-February 2025, which suggests continued growth in international sales volume for the company, as China Sunsine Chemical's products are used for tire production, they say. Shares are 0.9% higher at S$0.545. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1912 GMT - Chemours, the producer of fluorine-based refrigerants, specialty plastics and titanium dioxide pigments, is entering a more favorable environment, according to Mizuho in a research report. Analysts say one major tailwind is that industry stockpiling of the banned refrigerant Freon is normalizing amid the larger shift to next-gen refrigerants, of which Chemours is one of only two producers. The analysts also say that litigation surrounding PFAS, also known as forever chemicals, seems to be moving in the right direction. They believe a potential settlement in advance of the New Jersey PFAS trial, anticipated in spring/summer, would likely help limit the scope of Chemours' potential liability. Mizuho upgrades Chemours to outperform from neutral. Chemours is up 2.6% to $14.19. (sarina.isaacs@wsj.com)

1343 GMT - It looks like a surge in gold imports is still weighing on the U.S.'s trade deficit. In January, imports of the precious metal related to financial trading lifted the goods trade deficit to the highest level ever. The goods deficit declined about 5% last month, but gold still looks to be a big part of why the deficit is so high. Imports in the industrial-supplies category, which includes gold, remain elevated. "A big share of the overall deterioration in recent months reflects a boom in imports of gold in particular," Pantheon Macro economists write. "This matters because most trade in gold is excluded from the national accounts, given that it mostly reflects the transfer of existing financial assets rather than current economic activity." So it won't count against 1Q GDP. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1129 GMT - Indosuez Wealth Management maintains a cautious approach to duration, keeping a slight underweight on both U.S. and European government bonds. The wealth manager anticipates a slowdown in U.S. economic activity in 2025, without giving into pessimism, "as fears of recession seem exaggerated," it says in a note. It still expects two rate cuts by the Federal Reserve this year. The "nerve point" for markets has been tariffs policy. U.S. equity markets have largely moved past the "Trump Trade" and the optimistic expectations around Trump's second term, it says. Indosuez remains positive on the dollar, viewing it as a hedging asset in its portfolio and continues to favor gold. The 10-year Treasury yield is up 3.5 basis points at 4.371%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0802 GMT - The dollar edges lower as investors remain cautious amid uncertainty over U.S. tariffs and ahead of key U.S. economic data. President Trump on Wednesday announced a 25% tariff on automotive imports and is planning to impose retaliatory tariffs on U.S. trade partners on April 2. There are signs investors are becoming increasingly concerned about the prospect of higher inflation combined with weak growth--or stagflation--in the U.S. due to tariffs, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Given these concerns, the market will be closely monitoring U.S. core personal consumption expenditures price data 1230 GMT. It is the Federal Reserve's preferred measure of inflation. The DXY dollar index falls 0.1% to 104.283. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0434 GMT - Hyundai Motor is set to take a substantial earnings hit from President Trump's 25% auto tariff and a likely 5% average-selling-price increase to ease the tariff impact for cars selling in the U.S., Nomura analyst Angela Hong writes in a note. Hong lowers her 2025 operating-profit forecast for the South Korean carmaker by 18%, noting Hyundai's 48% reliance on imports for vehicle sales in the U.S. She also revises down her 2025 EPS for Hyundai by 13%. Nomura cuts its target price for the stock by 11% to KRW250,000 but maintains a buy rating, noting that investors have priced in much of the tariff impact. Shares are 4.2% lower at KRW203,500.(kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0230 GMT - China Sunsine Chemical's sales volumes are likely to sustain an uptrend, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report, as they raise its target price for the stock to S$0.63 from S$0.58, maintaining a buy rating. The rubber chemicals producer's sales in China are expected to improve, supported by the country's targeted stimulus measures to aid consumption recovery, the analysts say. Also, according to Tireworld, China's tire industry data service provider, the nation's rubber tire exports grew 3.3% on year in January-February 2025, which suggests continued growth in international sales volume for the company, as China Sunsine Chemical's products are used for tire production, they say. Shares are 0.9% higher at S$0.545. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0215 GMT - RHB IB downgrades Malaysia's oil & gas sector to neutral from overweight, citing prolonged uncertainties in the industry. Brent crude may remain at $75/bbl in 2025-2026, with OPEC+ gradually increasing output from April, analyst Sean Lim says in a note. The oil market's deficit could narrow to 0.7mbpd in 2025 from 1.4mbpd in 2024, and may turn into a surplus by 2026 if production hikes continue, he reckons. Geopolitical risks are in focus, with regional tensions and U.S. sanctions affecting supply, he says. Domestically, Petronas' shift to half-yearly financial reporting from a quarterly basis, cost-cutting measures, and uncertainties surrounding capital expenditure add to the sector's challenges, he says. RHB favors upstream service providers with strong maintenance exposure and global diversification for more stable earnings. The brokerage names Bumi Armada, Yinson Holdings, and Dayang Enterprise as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2243 GMT - Consensus expectations for Mainfreight's pretax profit to rise 15% in FY 2026 look optimistic to Forsyth Barr. Mainfreight's share price has fallen 8.3% this month so far, which reflects two big concerns of investors, it says. Tariff action has worsened the near-term economic outlook, while sea and air freight rates have both declined. "This backdrop suggests earnings growth in FY 2026 will be harder to achieve than current market expectations imply," analyst Andy Bowley says. Forsyth Barr expects Mainfreight's pretax profit to increase by 6% that year. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1903 GMT - Trump tariffs will likely be unbridled by concerns over how much harm they could do to business, Wolfe Research's Tobin Marcus and Chutong Zhu write. The President "is signaling significant pain tolerance, and we believe him," Marcus and Zhu say. They warn that the most recent announcement, a 25% levy on imported vehicles, will stack up with other duties, including ones to be revealed next week. "As with most of his tariff agenda, this looks like a play for long-term re-industrialization at the cost of short-term pain, and its scale suggests he is willing to endure some disruption." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1837 GMT - Tariffs on vehicles will stack up with other levies and "hit the auto industry hard," Wolfe Research's Tobin Marcus and Chutong Zhu write. They foresee the rate on European autos getting into the 40%-50% range after Liberation Day announcements next week. "For Mexico and Canada, reciprocal rates could realistically be in the 10% range, for an all-in rate around 35%," Marcus and Zhu say. "In general, we would characterize this as a hawkish outcome, in line with the President's preference for across-the-board tariffs at substantial rates, with minimal carveouts." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1824 GMT - Trump's 25% tariffs on imported vehicles includes "a minimal form of protection for the North American auto supply chain," Wolfe Research's Tobin Marcus and Chutong Zhu write. They say the tariffs exclude U.S. content of USMCA-compliant vehicle imports, among other carve-outs beneficial to the U.S.-Canada-Mexico agreement. Despite the exclusions, the tariffs' impact on North America's auto industry "will be major," since Trump wants to bring manufacturing back to the U.S. from its neighbors, Marcus and Zhu say. "The lack of a broad USMCA exemption from these auto tariffs also reinforces our expectations that the reciprocal tariffs next week will have no such exemption," they say. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1821 GMT - The exclusion of car parts from Trump's 25% tariffs on autos will likely be limited and won't do much to mitigate the levies' overall impact, Wolfe Research's Tobin Marcus and Chutong Zhu write. They say the White House cited $100 billion in new annual revenue, "which is not far below our revenue estimate for a 25% on all vehicles and parts," they say, adding that more information on the policy is needed to see exactly which parts are covered. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1818 GMT - Uncertainty around Trump tariffs "has been arguably more harmful than the tariffs themselves," Evercore analysts write. "Investors were looking to April 2 hoping to finally get their arms around the macro implications of Trump's tariffs, but we fear this won't be the whole story in the end." Evercore says the recent announcement of "secondary tariffs" punishing countries that buy Venezuelan oil "further reduces the likelihood that April 2 is the peak of trade uncertainty." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1759 GMT - German auto-parts supplier Robert Bosch says planned U.S. tariffs on imports of vehicles and select components are a challenge for the entire global auto industry. The company says it is evaluating how the proposed tariffs will affect its global and regional operations. Bosch prioritizes a local-for-local strategy, but works in an international production network and has global operations, it says. "It is essential to maintain a level-playing field for the European automotive industry, and especially for the supplier industry, so that it can assert itself in global competition," Bosch says. "We rely on political decision-makers to continue the dialogue to jointly find a solution to the current complex situation." (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1759 GMT - Renk Group is confident that the impact of U.S. tariffs on European manufacturers should be marginal for its business, Jefferies analysts Chloe Lemaire and Ben Brown say after hosting a conference with the German defense group's managers. This is because it has 90% of its supply chain localized in the U.S. The Renk America business is also considered a U.S.-based company with contracts for U.S. customers, they note. At the event, Chief Executive Alexander Sagel and Chief Financial Officer Anja Manz-Siebje said they expect to have more visibility on the potential share of Germany's growing defense budget that the company might capture after NATO releases its military requirements, probably in June, according to the analysts. Renk is working on the assumption that Germany's defense spending could progressively grow to 3.5% of gross domestic product by 2028-29, the analysts add. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: European shares fall at the end of week, dominated by tariff woes
data.description:
European shares dropped on Friday, at the end of a week marred by concerns over the global economic fallout of an intensifying trade war waged by U.S. President Donald Trump.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index fell 0.2% by 0811 GMT, after two straight days of declines and on track to shed 0.8% so far this week. Germany's trade-sensitive DAX index dropped 0.6%.

Ubisoft jumped 7.2% as the French video game maker said it had set up a subsidiary in which China's Tencent will invest 1.16 billion euros ($1.25 billion).

Europe's benchmark index retreated to two-week lows on Thursday after Trump's sweeping 25% import tariffs on the car industry raised jitters ahead of an April 2 deadline on reciprocal tariffs on U.S. trading partners.

Meanwhile, a survey showed German consumer sentiment is broadly unchanged heading into April, with a focus on saving, highlighting uncertainty among households.

In other company news, Deutsche Bank fell 2.7% as the bank extended CEO Christian Sewing's contract, while its deputy and another top executive will depart as part of a management revamp, cementing the leadership team of Germany's largest lender for the next phase of its turnaround.

data.title: Samsara Insider Sold Shares Worth $6,766,289, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
Sanjit Biswas, 10% Owner, Director, CEO, on March 25, 2025, sold 160,000 shares in Samsara for $6,766,289. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, Biswas Sanjit has control over a total of 2,993,417 Class A common shares of the company, with 999,628 shares held directly and 1,993,789 controlled indirectly.

SEC Filing:

www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1642896/000141588925009150/xslF345X05/form4-03272025_110305.xml

data.title: Hang Seng Dips 1.1% This Week
data.description:
The Hang Seng fell 152 points or 0.65% to close at 23,427 on Friday, snapping two-day gains amid broad-based losses.

The index lost 1.1% for the week, marking its third straight weekly fall as investors fled riskier assets over mounting concerns that new U.S. tariffs could hurt global growth.

President Trump plans to introduce a 25% tariff on auto imports and new reciprocal tariffs on key trading partners from April 2.

Caution also prevailed ahead of U.S. PCE inflation data later today and China’s March PMI figures next week.

However, declines were cushioned by Chinese President Xi Jinping’s fresh pledge to improve access and regain market confidence during a meeting with global business leaders in Beijing.

Meantime, Trump signaled a willingness to ease tariffs on China to enable a deal for ByteDance to sell TikTok.

Notable laggards included Semicon Manufacturing (-4.5%), Lenovo Group (-4.2%), and KE Hlds. (-3.0%), while auto stocks also fell, led by Nio (-7.2%) and Li Auto (-3.4%).

data.title: South African rand strengthens, Trump tariff worries linger
data.description:
South Africa's rand traded stronger early on Friday, capitalising on recent dollar weakness as concerns over U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs linger.

At 0745 GMT, the rand traded at 18.1925 against the dollar , about 0.3% firmer than its previous close.

Domestic investors are waiting to see the latest budget balance data due on Friday to gauge the health of Africa's most industrialised economy, amid local budget disputes and caution after Trump's announcement of tariffs on automotive imports to take effect from midnight on April 3.

"Should news break that the (coalition government) has made meaningful progress in settling on a budget, the positive sentiment might trigger an even more powerful surge in the ZAR and help it end the week below 18.20/dlr," said ETM Analytics in a research note.

On the stock market, the Top-40 index was last up 0.6%.

South Africa's benchmark 2030 government bond (ZAR2030=) was stronger in early deals, with the yield down 7 basis points to 9.08%.

data.title: Rizhao Port Investing 7 Billion Yuan in Port Transformation Project
data.description:
Rizhao Port subsidiary Jifa Co. will invest about 6.8 billion yuan in a port transformation project.

The first phase includes the construction of bulk cargo berths and the upgrade of infrastructure to boost capacity, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse. Construction is expected to take five years.

The project aims to strengthen Rizhao Port's role as a key logistics hub.

Shares of the port operator were down 2% Friday.
